title: Immigrants Raising Citizens: Undocumented Parents and Their Young Children By Hirokazu Yoshikawa Russell Sage Foundation. 2011. 208 pages. $24.95 paper
abstract: The repeated failure of immigration-related legislation in the U.S. Congress, and the simultaneous proliferation of mostly exclusionary immigration policies at state and local levels have led social scientists increasingly to speculate about the implications of parental legal status for the well-being and life chances of the children of immigrants. Very little research, however, has been brought to bear on this question, and, thus, Hirokazu Yoshikawa's book, Immigrants Raising Citizens: Undocumented Parents and Their Young Children, is a timely contribution, and the most thorough and systematic examination of the topic to date. The book is based on ethnographic and quantitative data from a longitudinal study of a birth cohort of 380 infants in New York City born to Chinese, Dominican and Mexican immigrant mothers as well as to U.S.-born African American mothers. The core of the book relies on ethnographic data, however, which focuses only on immigrant families. Because, as the author was surprised to find, most of the undocumented Chinese mothers sent their infants back to China to be cared for by grandparents, the parts of the book based on the ethnographic data primarily compare and contrast the experiences of Mexican mothers, who are overwhelmingly undocumented, with those of their largely legally resident Dominican counterparts.
